did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning and deep learning techniques, specifically an 'Active Learning V3 + approach' (likely referring to InceptionV3 with Active Learning) and transfer learning, to classify medical images of skin cancer (benign vs. malignant tumors). The study achieved an accuracy of 87.1% using the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a machine learning model ('Active Learning V3 + approach') for classifying skin cancer using the HAM10000 dataset. It includes specific sections for Introduction, Literature Review, Methods (data processing, model training), Results (accuracy of 87.1%, precision, recall, F1-scores), and Conclusion, which are characteristic of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Enhancing the Diagnosis of Skin Cancer Using Active Learning and Transfer Learning Techniques
journal: NTU Journal of Engineering and Technology (ICSDT2025) special issue
brief_summary: This study proposes a machine learning methodology to classify medical images of skin cancer into benign or malignant categories using the HAM10000 dataset. The authors employed an 'Active Learning V3 +' approach, which integrates transfer learning with the InceptionV3 architecture and data augmentation techniques. The model was trained and evaluated to enhance early detection capabilities, addressing the global health burden of skin cancer. Performance was assessed using metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and F1-score. The proposed model achieved an accuracy of 87.1%, outperforming conventional classification methods. The results suggest that combining InceptionV3 with active learning is effective for handling the complexities of medical image classification.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two binary classification categories in the HAM10000 dataset experiments.
Used as one of the two binary classification categories in the HAM10000 dataset experiments. Melanoma is specifically highlighted as the most surprising type of malignant skin cancer.

Mentioned in the abstract and introduction as a primary type of skin cancer and the most surprising of the two types (benign/malignant), but not explicitly listed as a distinct class in the binary classification task performed by the authors' model.

Mentioned in the literature review as one of the three primary forms of skin cancer, but not explicitly listed as a distinct class in the binary classification task performed by the authors' model.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin cancer images into benign or malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is 'classifying medical photos into benign or malignant tumors' using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic labels (benign vs. malignant). Performance metrics reported include accuracy (87.1% and 89% in different sections), precision, recall, and F1-score for the two classes (class 0: benign, class 1: malignant).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of skin cancer images used for training and evaluating the proposed Active Learning V3+ model. The dataset contains images categorized into benign and malignant classes.

use.training: Used to train the InceptionV3 model with transfer learning and active learning techniques.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the model's performance, achieving an accuracy of 87.1%.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate and report performance metrics including True Positives (1254), True Negatives (1556), False Positives (163), and False Negatives (183) for evaluating model classification accuracy.

To quantify model performance using Accuracy (87.1% and 89%), Precision, Recall, F1-score, Positive Predictive Value (PPV), Negative Predictive Value (NPV), False Positive Rate (FPR), False Negative Rate (FNR), Positive Likelihood Ratio (PLR), and Negative Likelihood Ratio (NLR).
To iteratively select the most uncertain or ambiguous samples from an unlabeled pool of data for labeling, thereby refining the model and improving efficiency and correctness.
To monitor the evolution of model accuracy and loss over epochs to understand the learning trajectory and identify convergence or overfitting.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, and compared to other methods based on these point estimates without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The proposed model achieved an overall accuracy of 89.07% (also cited as 87.1% in the abstract). It outperformed baseline methods including EfficientNet B4 (87.8%), FRCNN (88.50%), and MobileNet (88.57%). The model was evaluated on a binary classification task distinguishing benign from malignant tumors.

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning approach; base model weights were frozen during the first training phase to leverage learned features. The paper mentions using pretrained ImageNet weights in the context of related work (EfficientNets), but for the proposed model, it specifies freezing the base InceptionV3 weights.

architecture_details.modifications: The model was extended by adding new layers to tailor it to the specific task of tumor classification. The last layers were modified for binary classification (benign vs. malignant).
architecture_details.active_learning_integration: Active Learning V3+ approach was employed. The model iteratively queries the most uncertain samples from an unlabeled pool of data to refine the model.

hyperparameters.regularization: Mentioned generally that regularization methods (e.g., dropout, L2 regularization) can help generalize, but specific values for the proposed model are not reported.
hyperparameters.data_augmentation: Images were augmented by applying rotations to artificially increase dataset size and variety.
hyperparameters.normalization: Images were normalized to scale pixel values.

training_details.training_schedule: The model was trained on an initial subset of data. Active learning was incorporated as a dynamic part of the training process. The number of iterations (epochs) was determined through experimentation to balance underfitting and overfitting, with training history visualizations used to monitor accuracy and loss evolution.

classification_or_output_head.details: The SoftMax layer was altered to identify binary classification (benign vs. malignant tumors). The model outputs predictions for two classes: Class 0 (Benign) and Class 1 (Malignant).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Active Learning V3 + (InceptionV3 with Active Learning)
preprocessing_techniques: Data organization: File paths combined into data frames for each category and set, labeled appropriately.
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA): Visual representations (bar and pie charts) generated to check class balance.
Data Augmentation: Images augmented by applying rotations to increase dataset size and variety.
Normalization: Images normalized to scale pixel values for stable and faster training.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Images were augmented by applying rotations to artificially increase the dataset size and variety.

conclusions: The proposed 'Active Learning V3 +' approach, which integrates InceptionV3 with active learning and transfer learning techniques, achieved an accuracy of 89.07% in classifying skin cancer images into benign and malignant categories.
The proposed model outperformed baseline methods, including EfficientNet B4 (87.8%), FRCNN (88.50%), and MobileNet (88.57%).

Active learning enhances model performance by improving efficiency and correctness while decreasing the requirement for large amounts of labeled data through the active querying of ambiguous or instructive samples.

The study demonstrates that combining transfer learning with active learning is effective for handling the complexities of medical image classification and enhancing the early diagnosis of skin cancer.
The model achieved high precision and recall scores for both benign (Class 0: Precision 0.90, Recall 0.91) and malignant (Class 1: Precision 0.88, Recall 0.87) classes.
important_remarks: The study is limited to a binary classification task (benign vs. malignant), ignoring the multi-class distinctions (e.g., melanoma vs. basal cell carcinoma) present in the HAM10000 dataset, which limits clinical granularity.
The paper does not report the specific train/validation/test split ratios or the exact number of images used per class, preventing assessment of data leakage risks or class imbalance handling.
Data augmentation was limited to rotation only, which may be insufficient for robust generalization in medical imaging compared to more extensive augmentation strategies.
The model was evaluated exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset; the lack of external validation on independent datasets raises concerns about generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the reported performance improvements over baseline models.
The paper does not specify the hardware environment or computational resources used, making reproducibility and deployment feasibility difficult to assess.

The 'Active Learning V3+' methodology lacks detailed description of the query strategy (e.g., uncertainty sampling, margin sampling) and the initial labeled pool size, which are critical for evaluating the active learning component's efficacy.
